# Crypto Intelligence Dashboard
## User Manual — Version 1.0

---

&nbsp;

&nbsp;

&nbsp;

**Prepared by:** SB Finance Research  
**Platform:** Streamlit Cloud  
**Coverage:** 688 Non-Stablecoin Digital Assets  
**Data Frequency:** Daily OHLCV with 70+ Pre-Computed Technical Indicators  
**Universe:** Global Crypto Markets — Binance, Major CEX Feeds  

&nbsp;

&nbsp;

&nbsp;

---

*This manual is intended for analysts, portfolio managers, and quantitative researchers seeking to navigate, interpret, and act on signals generated by the Crypto Intelligence Dashboard. The platform consolidates market structure analysis, return attribution, technical regime classification, and forward-return base-rate research into a single unified interface.*

---

&nbsp;

## Quick Start

1. Open the dashboard at your deployment URL
2. The sidebar loads automatically on first visit
3. Select your desired Return Period from the sidebar — default is One Year
4. Select the Cap Tiers relevant to your coverage universe
5. Navigate across the six analytical tabs using the tab bar beneath the KPI header
6. All charts, tables, and metrics update in real time as sidebar selections change
7. Hover over any data point, bubble, or bar for a detailed breakdown tooltip
8. Expand any table section using the arrow toggle beneath each chart

---

&nbsp;

## 1. Platform Overview

The Crypto Intelligence Dashboard is a professional-grade market analytics platform built for systematic analysis of the digital asset universe. It is designed around three core analytical workflows that mirror the research process of a quantitative trading desk.

The first workflow is top-down market scanning, where the analyst begins at the macro level — examining which capitalisation segments are generating alpha, how volatility is distributed across the universe, and which coins are outperforming or underperforming the median over the selected horizon. This is the function of the Market Overview and Tier Breakdown tabs.

The second workflow is single-asset deep dive analysis, where the analyst drills into a specific coin to examine its price structure, momentum profile, and current technical regime across multiple independent signals simultaneously. This is the function of the Coin Deep Dive tab.

The third workflow is cross-asset intelligence, where the analyst identifies structural relationships between assets — either through historical return co-movement or through current technical positioning similarity. This is the function of the Return Similarity, Regime Similarity, and Forward Return Lab tabs.

The platform is not a trading system and does not generate buy or sell signals. It is a research and analytical environment designed to surface information, identify patterns, and support the analyst's own decision-making process with rigorous quantitative context.

---

&nbsp;

## 2. Data Infrastructure

The underlying dataset covers 688 non-stablecoin digital assets sourced from major centralised exchange feeds. All stablecoins — assets algorithmically pegged to fiat currencies or other reference assets — are excluded from the universe at the data loading stage to ensure that all analytical outputs reflect price-discovery assets only.

The dataset is stored and served in the Apache Parquet columnar format from a dedicated HuggingFace dataset repository. Parquet encoding reduces the data footprint by approximately eighty percent relative to the raw CSV representation, enabling fast streaming directly into the platform without intermediate storage.

Data is loaded once per session and held entirely in memory using the Polars DataFrame library, a high-performance columnar query engine optimised for analytical workloads. All filtering, aggregation, and computation happens in-process. No external API calls are made after the initial data load.

The session cache is maintained by Streamlit's caching layer. Once loaded, all tab navigation and sidebar interactions are instant. Reloading the browser page triggers a fresh data fetch.

The dataset covers daily granularity. Each row represents one trading day for one asset and includes the full OHLCV record alongside seventy pre-computed technical indicators spanning trend, momentum, volatility, volume, and directional signal families. Forward return labels for the one, two, three, five, and seven day horizons are pre-computed and embedded in the dataset for use in the Forward Return Lab.

The dataset is updated periodically. The date range displayed in the sidebar header reflects the actual coverage of the loaded data. All return calculations and regime snapshots are anchored to the latest available date in the dataset, not the current calendar date.

---

&nbsp;

## 3. Sidebar Controls

The sidebar is the global control panel for the entire platform. Every selection made in the sidebar propagates immediately to all six tabs, all KPI cards, and all charts. There is no apply or refresh button — the dashboard is fully reactive.

**Return Period**

The Return Period selector determines the historical window used for all return calculations displayed across the platform. Six options are available: one month, three months, six months, and one year. Assets that were listed after the selected cutoff date are automatically excluded from return-period metrics, as they do not have sufficient price history to compute a valid return for the full window. The number of excluded assets is displayed in the sixth KPI card.

Analysts covering short-term momentum strategies should use the one-month or three-month window. Analysts conducting medium-term trend or cycle analysis should use the six-month or one-year window.

**Cap Tier Filter**

The Cap Tier filter segments the universe into five market capitalisation bands. Each tier can be independently toggled on or off. The five tiers are Mega Cap, Large Cap, Mid Cap, Small Cap, and Micro Cap, covering the full spectrum from the most liquid blue-chip assets to emerging low-liquidity tokens.

When multiple tiers are selected, all charts and metrics reflect the combined universe. When a single tier is isolated, the platform functions as a focused screen for that segment of the market.

Analysts focused on institutional-grade liquidity should isolate Mega and Large Cap. Analysts seeking asymmetric return opportunities in less efficient segments of the market should include Small and Micro Cap, noting the elevated volatility and liquidity risk inherent to those tiers.

**Volatility Clip**

The Volatility Clip control sets the maximum daily volatility value displayed on the x-axis of the Risk vs Return scatter chart. Assets with realised daily volatility above the clip threshold are plotted at the clip boundary rather than their true value. This prevents extreme volatility outliers — common in the Micro Cap tier — from compressing the scale and obscuring the distribution of the broader universe.

**Maximum Return Clip**

The Maximum Return Clip control sets the upper bound of the y-axis on the Risk vs Return scatter chart. Identical in logic to the Volatility Clip, this control prevents parabolic return outliers from dominating the vertical scale and rendering the chart unreadable for the majority of assets.

---

&nbsp;

## 4. KPI Header Row

Six key performance indicator cards are displayed persistently at the top of the dashboard across all tabs. They provide an at-a-glance summary of the selected universe under the active return period.

**Total Market Capitalisation** displays the aggregate last-known market capitalisation of all assets within the selected cap tier filter. This figure reflects the most recent available market cap snapshot and does not change with the return period selection, as it is a point-in-time balance sheet figure rather than a flow metric.

**Median Return** displays the median return percentage across all qualifying assets for the selected period. The median is used rather than the mean to reduce sensitivity to extreme outliers in either direction, which are common in digital asset markets.

**Best Performer** displays the ticker symbol of the asset with the highest return over the selected period, alongside the magnitude of that return. This card identifies the top alpha generator in the current universe selection.

**Worst Performer** displays the ticker symbol of the asset with the lowest return over the selected period. In bear markets or risk-off regimes, this card highlights the largest capital destruction events in the universe.

**Percentage Positive** displays the share of qualifying assets that generated a positive return over the selected period, expressed as a percentage and a coin count fraction. A reading above fifty percent indicates a broadly positive market environment for the selected universe. A reading below fifty percent indicates that the majority of assets in the selected segment are in drawdown over the period.

**Coins with Period Data** displays the count of assets that have sufficient listing history to compute a valid return for the selected period, alongside the number of assets excluded due to insufficient history. This card is particularly relevant when shorter-listed assets in the Small and Micro Cap tiers are included in the universe.

---

&nbsp;

## 5. Tab One — Market Overview

The Market Overview tab is the primary top-down market structure screen. Its central feature is the Risk versus Return scatter chart, which maps the full selected universe across two dimensions simultaneously — realised daily volatility on the horizontal axis and period return on the vertical axis. Each asset is represented as a bubble scaled logarithmically by market capitalisation, ensuring that both large-cap and micro-cap assets remain visible within the same chart frame.

The chart is divided into four quadrants by two reference lines. The horizontal line at zero separates profitable assets above from loss-making assets below. The vertical gold dashed line marks the median daily volatility of the displayed universe, separating below-median risk assets on the left from above-median risk assets on the right.

The analytically ideal quadrant is the upper-left — assets generating positive returns at below-median volatility. These represent the strongest risk-adjusted performers in the universe. The lower-right quadrant represents the worst risk-adjusted positioning — high volatility combined with negative returns, characteristic of assets experiencing structural deterioration or speculative unwinding.

Hovering over any bubble displays the full return breakdown across all available periods, alongside the realised volatility, a proxy Sharpe ratio, and the last known market capitalisation. This allows rapid cross-period return attribution without navigating away from the overview.

Beneath the scatter chart, the Winners Table expander provides a fully ranked tabular view of all assets in the current selection, sorted by the active return period. The active period column is highlighted in gold for rapid identification. A condensed Top Five and Bottom Five summary is displayed below the full table for quick reference.

---

&nbsp;

## 6. Tab Two — Tier Breakdown

The Tier Breakdown tab provides a comparative decomposition of performance and risk characteristics across the five capitalisation segments. Three bar charts are displayed side by side: coin count per tier, average period return per tier, and median daily volatility per tier.

The average return chart uses green bars for positive average returns and red bars for negative, providing an immediate visual read on which capitalisation segments are in aggregate bull or bear territory for the selected period. The volatility chart uses a colour gradient from blue at the low end to pink at the high end, reflecting the well-documented positive correlation between decreasing market capitalisation and increasing daily volatility in digital asset markets.

Below the bar charts, the Return Distribution Violin Plot provides a statistically richer view of the return landscape. Rather than the point estimate of the average, the violin plot displays the full probability distribution of returns for each tier. The width of the violin at any return level is proportional to the number of assets posting returns at that level. The inner box covers the interquartile range, and the centre line marks the median.

Wide violins indicate broad participation in a return level — gains or losses distributed across many assets. Narrow violins with extended tails indicate concentrated performance — a small number of outlier assets driving the tier average while the majority of the tier remains flat or negative. This distinction is critical for portfolio construction, as concentrated performance within a tier implies idiosyncratic rather than systematic factor exposure.

---

&nbsp;

## 7. Tab Three — Coin Deep Dive

The Coin Deep Dive tab provides a comprehensive single-asset analytical view combining price action, momentum indicators, volume analysis, and a real-time technical regime assessment.

**Asset Selection and Chart Configuration**

The asset selector is populated with all coins within the currently selected cap tier filter, sorted by market capitalisation in descending order. Three chart types are available: line chart for clean price action visualisation, candlestick for full OHLC session structure, and OHLC bar format. The date range selector controls the historical window displayed, from one month to the full available history.

**Four-Panel Chart Structure**

The price panel occupies the upper fifty-two percent of the chart frame. In line mode, a twenty-day simple moving average overlay appears in gold when sufficient history is present within the selected range. In candlestick and OHLC modes, sessions are colour-coded with teal for advancing days and red for declining days.

The RSI panel displays the fourteen-period Relative Strength Index. The panel includes shaded reference zones — gold above seventy for overbought conditions and red below thirty for oversold conditions. RSI readings at the extremes of these zones indicate that near-term price extension may be statistically above its historical norm, increasing the probability of mean reversion. RSI within the thirty to seventy neutral band provides no directional edge in isolation.

The MACD panel displays the Moving Average Convergence Divergence indicator in three components simultaneously: the MACD line representing the spread between fast and slow exponential moving averages, the signal line as a smoothed average of the MACD, and the histogram representing the signed gap between the two. A histogram expanding in the positive direction indicates accelerating bullish momentum. Histogram compression toward zero, followed by a cross below the signal line, has historically preceded near-term price deterioration.

The volume panel displays daily traded volume in USD with directional colouring — teal bars on advancing sessions and red bars on declining sessions. Volume expansion on advancing days confirms institutional participation in the move. Volume expansion on declining days signals distribution — selling pressure with broad market participation. Volume contraction across both advancing and declining days indicates consolidation or reduced conviction in either direction.

**Regime State Cards**

Six regime state cards are displayed beneath the chart, each providing an independent technical assessment of the asset's current positioning. These cards are sourced from the latest available data row for the selected asset and represent a real-time regime snapshot.

The RSI Regime card classifies current momentum as oversold, neutral, or overbought based on the latest RSI reading. The Trend Strength card classifies directional conviction as ranging, trending, or strongly trending based on the ADX reading. The Trend Direction card requires both the Supertrend and Parabolic SAR indicators to agree before classifying the direction as bullish or bearish — if the two signals diverge, the card returns a mixed classification indicating a transitional regime. The Bollinger Band Position card indicates where the current price sits within the band structure relative to its statistical distribution. The EMA Stack card assesses the alignment of the nine, twenty-one, and fifty period exponential moving averages — a fully aligned bull stack indicates sustained medium-term momentum in the upward direction. The Surge Score card displays a composite momentum and breakout score aggregating multiple signals into a single dimensionless index.

When four or more regime cards align in the same directional classification, the technical conviction for that direction is considered elevated. When signals are broadly mixed, the asset is in a transitional regime with no exploitable directional edge from technical analysis alone.

---

&nbsp;

## 8. Tab Four — Return Similarity Engine

The Return Similarity Engine identifies which assets in the selected universe have historically exhibited the strongest co-movement with a designated anchor asset, based on the statistical correlation of daily logarithmic returns over a user-selected lookback window.

**Methodology**

Pearson correlation is computed on daily log returns for each asset pair sharing the anchor's observation window. Log returns are used rather than simple price returns because they are additive across time, symmetric around zero, and more consistent with the distributional assumptions underlying correlation analysis.

An asset scores highly on return similarity if it has tended to rise and fall on the same calendar days as the anchor — regardless of the magnitude of its absolute price movement or its long-term trend direction. A coin that simply drifted upward over months while the anchor was flat would score poorly, as there is no day-to-day co-movement despite a superficially similar directional trend.

The Same Direction Percentage provides a non-parametric complement to Pearson correlation. It measures the fraction of shared trading days on which both assets moved in the same direction. A reading of fifty percent represents random co-movement. Readings above seventy-five percent indicate a robust directional relationship that is unlikely to be attributable to coincidence.

Statistical significance is assessed using the p-value of the Pearson correlation test. Only correlations with p-values below five percent are considered statistically reliable. The KPI card displaying significant results out of ten provides an immediate assessment of the robustness of the similarity cluster.

**Normalised Price Overlay**

All assets in the similarity cluster are rebased to one hundred at the start of the selected window. This removes price-scale differences and renders co-movement directly comparable on a single chart frame. A high Pearson correlation should produce visually overlapping lines in the rebased chart. Points of divergence in the rebased chart identify specific dates and market conditions under which the correlation broke down — highly valuable information for understanding the conditions under which the relationship is and is not reliable.

**Rolling Ninety-Day Correlation**

The rolling correlation chart is the most analytically important view in this tab. It decomposes the single summary correlation coefficient into its time series, revealing whether the relationship between the anchor and its peers is structurally stable or regime-dependent.

A stable line near or above zero point eight indicates a persistent structural relationship that is likely driven by shared fundamental exposure — the same investor base, similar on-chain utility, or correlated treasury flows. Such relationships are more reliable for hedging, pair trading, and portfolio construction applications.

A correlation that collapses during drawdown periods indicates that the relationship holds only in benign market conditions and breaks down under stress. This is a common pattern in digital asset markets, where cross-asset correlations frequently converge toward one during sharp risk-off events regardless of the assets' underlying characteristics.

**Seasonal Return Heatmaps**

The month-by-year return heatmaps display the total return for each calendar month across all available years for both the anchor and its best-matched peer. Reading across a single row — for example, all October cells — reveals whether an asset exhibits a statistically consistent calendar month bias. Patterns that appear consistently across multiple years rather than isolated years are more likely to reflect a genuine seasonal effect rather than sampling noise.

The differential heatmap beneath the paired views shows the month-by-month return spread between the anchor and its best match. Persistent teal cells in a given month indicate the anchor systematically outperforms its peer in that month. Persistent red cells indicate the peer systematically outperforms.

---

&nbsp;

## 9. Tab Five — Regime Similarity Engine

The Regime Similarity Engine addresses a fundamentally different analytical question from the Return Similarity Engine. While the latter looks backward and asks which assets have moved together historically, the Regime Similarity Engine looks at the present moment and asks which assets are currently in the same technical state as the anchor.

**The Eleven-Feature Technical Vector**

Each asset's current technical posture is represented as an eleven-dimensional feature vector constructed from the most recent available data row. The eleven features are the Relative Strength Index, Bollinger Band percentage position, Average Directional Index, positive and negative directional indicators, Supertrend direction signal, Parabolic SAR trend signal, Chaikin Money Flow, Money Flow Index, Aroon Oscillator, and the EMA stack alignment score across the nine, twenty-one, and fifty period exponential moving averages.

Each feature is z-score normalised across the full universe before similarity computation. This normalisation ensures that scale-dependent indicators measured in absolute units do not dominate scale-independent indicators measured in binary or bounded ranges.

**Cosine Similarity**

Similarity between two assets' technical vectors is measured using cosine similarity, which computes the angular distance between the two vectors in eleven-dimensional feature space. A cosine similarity of one indicates perfect technical alignment across all eleven features. A value of zero indicates orthogonal — completely uncorrelated — technical positioning.

Cosine similarity is preferred over Euclidean distance for this application because it captures directional alignment rather than absolute magnitude, which is the analytically relevant dimension when assessing whether two assets are in the same regime.

**Feature Radar Chart**

The radar chart overlays the anchor asset's normalised feature vector against its best-matched peer's vector. Spokes that overlap closely confirm co-positioning on that indicator. Spokes that diverge identify the specific dimensions on which the two assets differ despite an overall high cosine similarity score. For example, two assets may share similar RSI and ADX readings but diverge on Chaikin Money Flow, suggesting that one is receiving stronger institutional capital allocation despite similar price momentum — a potentially exploitable divergence.

**Practical Applications**

The Regime Similarity Engine supports three primary analytical use cases. The first is regime-aware pair trading, where two assets identified as technically co-positioned today but historically divergent in returns present a potential relative value opportunity. The second is portfolio regime concentration risk assessment, where holdings that share near-identical regime vectors will react identically to the next macro catalyst — an often-unrecognised source of hidden correlation in crypto portfolios. The third is regime transition monitoring, where assets that have recently migrated from one technical cluster to another are in transition — either breaking out of a ranging regime into a trend, or deteriorating from a bullish momentum regime into a bearish one.

---

&nbsp;

## 10. Tab Six — Forward Return Lab

The Forward Return Lab is a conditional base-rate analysis tool. It answers the following analytical question: given that a selected asset is currently in a specific RSI and ADX regime, what has the distribution of subsequent returns historically looked like over the following one, two, three, five, and seven trading days?

This is not a prediction engine. It is a historical probability assessment — the quantitative equivalent of asking, on a trading desk, how this setup has played out in the past before sizing a position. Past regime outcomes do not guarantee future returns. They shift the analyst's prior probability estimate and provide quantitative context for position sizing and risk management decisions.

**Regime Matching**

Historical matches are identified by scanning the full price history of the selected asset for dates on which the RSI and ADX readings fell within user-defined tolerance bands around the current readings. The RSI tolerance and ADX tolerance sliders control the width of these bands. Tighter bands produce fewer but more precise historical matches. Wider bands produce larger sample sizes but introduce matches from less similar regimes, reducing the signal-to-noise ratio of the resulting distribution.

A minimum of fifteen to twenty matched observations is recommended before drawing analytical conclusions from the distribution. Below this threshold, the sample is too small for the distributional statistics to be reliable, and individual outlier events will disproportionately influence the median and percentile readings.

**Forward Return Distribution**

The box plot displays the statistical distribution of forward returns at each of the five horizons. The interquartile range — the middle fifty percent of outcomes — is represented by the box. The median return is marked by the centre line. The whiskers extend to the fifth and ninety-fifth percentiles. Points beyond the whiskers are individual outlier observations.

A distribution box sitting entirely above zero across multiple horizons indicates that this regime has historically been associated with sustained near-term price appreciation. A box straddling zero indicates that the regime is historically inconclusive — exits have been roughly evenly distributed between positive and negative outcomes. A box sitting below zero signals that this regime has historically preceded near-term price deterioration.

The percentage of matched observations with positive five-day returns — displayed in the fourth KPI card — is a particularly useful summary statistic for assessing the directional skew of the distribution. A reading above sixty percent constitutes a meaningful bullish historical skew. A reading below forty percent constitutes a meaningful bearish historical skew.

**Surge Score Predictive Analysis**

The scatter chart at the base of the tab tests whether the Surge Score — the platform's composite momentum and breakout indicator — carries incremental predictive value within the identified regime. Each point on the scatter represents one matched historical date. The horizontal axis shows the Surge Score recorded on that date. The vertical axis shows the five-day return that followed.

An ordinary least squares regression line is fitted to the scatter with its correlation coefficient displayed. A coefficient above zero point three indicates that within this regime, higher Surge Score readings have historically preceded stronger five-day returns — the momentum signal adds value. A flat or negative regression slope indicates that Surge Score is not informative for this asset in this regime and should not be used as a sizing or timing input.

---

&nbsp;

## 11. Indicator Reference

**Trend Indicators**

Exponential Moving Averages are computed across the nine, twenty-one, fifty, one hundred, and two hundred period windows. Their relative alignment — bull stack when ascending, bear stack when descending — provides a multi-timeframe momentum confirmation signal. Simple Moving Averages are computed at the fifty and two hundred period windows and are used primarily for longer-term trend identification.

The Supertrend indicator is an ATR-adjusted trend-following signal that produces a binary directional output — bullish or bearish — with a dynamic trailing stop level. It is most reliable in trending market conditions and generates false signals at elevated frequency during ranging or low-ADX regimes.

The Parabolic SAR is a trend-following and trailing stop indicator. It is used in conjunction with Supertrend — both signals must agree for the platform to classify trend direction as confirmed. A divergence between the two signals is classified as mixed, indicating a transitional regime.

The Ichimoku Cloud system provides trend direction, momentum, and support and resistance context through four component lines: Tenkan-Sen, Kijun-Sen, Senkou Span A, and Senkou Span B.

**Momentum Indicators**

The Relative Strength Index measures the speed and magnitude of recent price changes on a bounded zero to one hundred scale. Readings above seventy are conventionally classified as overbought. Readings below thirty are classified as oversold. The RSI is most useful as a regime classifier rather than a standalone entry signal.

Stochastic Oscillator K and D lines measure the position of the closing price relative to the high-low range over a specified lookback period. Williams Percent Range and Commodity Channel Index provide related but independently derived momentum perspectives.

Rate of Change indicators at the nine, fourteen, and twenty-one period windows measure the percentage price change over the respective lookback. The True Strength Index is a double-smoothed momentum oscillator less susceptible to short-term noise than standard ROC-based measures.

The MACD system uses the standard twelve, twenty-six, and nine period parameterisation.

**Volatility Indicators**

Bollinger Bands are constructed as two standard deviation envelopes around a twenty-period simple moving average. The Band Width measures the absolute spread between the upper and lower bands — a contraction in Band Width frequently precedes a volatility expansion. The Bollinger Band Percentage position is the normalised position of the current price within the band.

Keltner Channels use ATR-based envelopes rather than standard deviation, making them less sensitive to individual spike sessions. Donchian Channels mark the highest high and lowest low over a specified lookback period and are used for breakout identification.

The Average True Range measures realised volatility on an absolute price basis and is used as an input to several other indicators including Supertrend and position-sizing frameworks.

**Volume Indicators**

On-Balance Volume accumulates volume in the direction of daily price movement, providing a running total of buying and selling pressure. The Accumulation Distribution Line weights volume by the position of the close within the daily range, distinguishing between sessions that close near their high versus near their low despite similar volume totals.

Chaikin Money Flow and Money Flow Index both measure the balance of capital flow into and out of an asset. CMF readings above zero indicate net accumulation. MFI readings above eighty indicate overbought volume conditions. Volume Rate of Change measures the momentum of trading activity independently of price.

VWAP — Volume Weighted Average Price — represents the average price at which the asset has traded over the session weighted by volume, and serves as a reference benchmark for institutional execution quality assessment.

**Directional Indicators**

The ADX system comprises three components: the Average Directional Index measuring trend strength irrespective of direction, the Positive Directional Indicator measuring upward price movement pressure, and the Negative Directional Indicator measuring downward pressure. The spread between the positive and negative directional indicators signals the balance of directional conviction.

The Aroon system measures how recently the asset made a new high or low within the lookback period. The Aroon Oscillator — the difference between Aroon Up and Aroon Down — is a signed directional signal ranging from negative one hundred to positive one hundred.

**Forward Return Labels**

Pre-computed forward return labels for the one, two, three, five, and seven calendar day horizons are embedded in the dataset. These labels represent the actual subsequent returns observed in the historical data and are used exclusively in the Forward Return Lab for base-rate analysis.

**Surge Score**

The Surge Score is a proprietary composite indicator aggregating momentum, breakout, and directional signals across multiple indicator families into a single dimensionless index. Elevated readings indicate that several independent signals are simultaneously registering above their historical norms, suggesting a statistically unusual concentration of momentum or breakout characteristics.

---

&nbsp;

## 12. Analytical Limitations and Disclaimers

The Total Market Capitalisation figure displayed in the KPI header always reflects the latest available market cap snapshot for each asset. It does not represent a point-in-time historical market cap as of the start of the selected return period. Analysts should not use this figure for historical market cap calculations.

Return period calculations require assets to have been listed and trading for the full duration of the selected window. Assets listed after the period start date are excluded from return metrics. The exclusion count is displayed in the sixth KPI card. Analysts working with short return periods such as one month will observe higher inclusion counts. Analysts selecting longer periods will observe more exclusions, particularly in the Small and Micro Cap tiers where many assets have shorter listing histories.

The regime snapshot used in the Regime Similarity and Forward Return Lab tabs reflects the latest available data row for each asset. Given that the dataset is updated periodically and not in real time, the regime snapshot may be several days to several weeks behind the live market. The date range displayed in the sidebar header reflects the actual data coverage. Analysts should account for this potential lag when interpreting regime-based outputs relative to current market conditions.

Pearson correlation as used in the Return Similarity Engine is a measure of linear co-movement. Non-linear relationships, threshold effects, and conditional correlations — common in digital asset markets — will not be fully captured by the Pearson coefficient. The Rolling Ninety-Day Correlation chart provides a partial remedy by revealing temporal instability in the linear relationship.

The Forward Return Lab outputs are historical base rates, not forward-looking forecasts. The conditional return distributions are computed from the historical data embedded in the dataset. Past regime outcomes are informative but not predictive. Market structure, liquidity conditions, regulatory environment, and macro factors can and do shift the forward return distribution relative to its historical analogue. All outputs from the Forward Return Lab should be treated as probabilistic context for decision-making rather than directional recommendations.

This platform is a research and analytical tool. It does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, or a recommendation to buy or sell any digital asset. All analytical outputs are for informational and research purposes only.
